coredata.dc:title: Novel S, N-doped carbon quantum dot-based "off-on" fluorescent sensor for silver ion and cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Sen
Zhao, Xinyi
Zhu, Fawei
Chen, Miao
Wu, Zhiliang
song, Xiangzhi
Yang, Hua
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CQDs) as highly selective fluorescent probe for silver ion (Ag+) and cysteine (Cys) detection were designed and synthesized directly from citric acid and thiamine hydrochloride via a one-step hydrothermal protocol in 63.8% quantum yield. This probe enabled selective detection of Ag+ with a linear range of 0–10 and 10–250μM and a limit of detection of 0.40μM with respect to the variation in fluorescence induced by target concentration and electron-transfer from S,N-CQDs to Ag+. Furthermore, S,N-CQDs/Ag+ fluorescence can be effectively recovered by virtue of a specific reaction of Cys with silver ion. This fluorescence “turn-on” protocol was applied to determine Cys with two linear ranges of 0–10 and 10–120μM as well as a detection limit of 0.35μM. The corresponding cell experiments were also performed, indicating that the prepared S,N-CQDs possessed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Ultimately, the practicality and viability of this fluorescent probe were demonstrated through the analysis of silver ion in real river water and human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Fluorescent probe
Silver ions
Cysteine
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of the concentration of Cys
Fluorescence quenching of S,N-CQDs by Ag+
Fluorescence recovery of S,N-CQDs by Cys
Cytotoxicity studies of S,N-CQDs and fluorescence imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme for the synthesis of S, N-CQDs by CA and VB1; QY of S, N-CQDs as a function of (b) pH of reaction solution; (c) ratio of VB1 to CA; (d) reaction temperature; (e) reaction time.
(a) TEM and size distribution (inset) images of S, N-CQDs. (b) EDS spectrum of S, N-CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of S, N-CQDs. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of S,N-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of S, N-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), O1s (c) and N1s (d).

(a) Photoluminescence spectrum of S, N-CQDs; (b) fluorescence intensity at 424nm upon excitation at 370nm as a function of pH; (c) effect of ionic strengths on fluorescence intensity of S, N-CQDs with various concentrations of NaNO3 (0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0M); (d) the relationship between fluorescence intensity and time under natural illumination.

(a) Selectivity for various metal ions. All competing ion solutions were 0.01M. (b) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of S, N-CQDs in the presence of increasing Ag+ concentrations (0–250μM) in the 0.01M PBS at pH 7.0. (c) The relationship between F-F0 and Ag+ from 10 to 250μM. (d) The relationship between F-F0 and Ag+ from 0 to 10μM. F and F0 are the FL intensities of S, N-CQDs at 424nm in the presence and absence of Ag+, respectively.

(a) Selectivity for amino acids. All competing amino acid solutions were 0.001M. (b) Representative FL emission spectra of S, N-CQDs containing Ag+ (50μM) in the presence of increasing Cys concentrations (0–120μM) in the 0.01M PBS at pH 7.0. (c) The relationship between F-F0 and Cys from 10 to 120μM. (d) The relationship between F-F0 and Cys from 0 to 10μM. F and F0 are the FL intensities of S, N-CQDs at 424nm in the presence and absence of Cys, respectively.
Effects of S, N-CQDs at different concentrations on the viability of Hela cells for 24h.
Fluorescence images of HeLa cells excited at 370nm. (a) bright field, (b) blue channel fluorescence images, (c) overlay of bright field and fluorescence images, Scale bar: 50µm.
Comparison of detection performance of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection.
Comparison of detection performance of different fluorescent probes for Cys detection.
Determination of Ag+ in natural water samples.
Determination of Cys in human serums.

head.ce:title: Novel S, N-doped carbon quantum dot-based "off-on" fluorescent sensor for silver ion and cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Utilization of Manganese Resources, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Utilization of Manganese Resources, Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CQDs) as highly selective fluorescent probe for silver ion (Ag+) and cysteine (Cys) detection were designed and synthesized directly from citric acid and thiamine hydrochloride via a one-step hydrothermal protocol in 63.8% quantum yield. This probe enabled selective detection of Ag+ with a linear range of 0–10 and 10–250μM and a limit of detection of 0.40μM with respect to the variation in fluorescence induced by target concentration and electron-transfer from S,N-CQDs to Ag+. Furthermore, S,N-CQDs/Ag+ fluorescence can be effectively recovered by virtue of a specific reaction of Cys with silver ion. This fluorescence “turn-on” protocol was applied to determine Cys with two linear ranges of 0–10 and 10–120μM as well as a detection limit of 0.35μM. The corresponding cell experiments were also performed, indicating that the prepared S,N-CQDs possessed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Ultimately, the practicality and viability of this fluorescent probe were demonstrated through the analysis of silver ion in real river water and human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their distinct advantages including good biocompatibility [1], low toxicity [2] and good photostability [3], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received extensive attention as promising carbon materials in recent years. CQDs have been of significance in optical detection probes [4], bioimaging [5], biosensing [6], nanomedicine [7], light emitting diode materials and visible light-active photocatalysts [8,9] etc. CQDs, nano-spherical forms of carbon, could be facilely prepared through a variety of methods, such as burning, hydrothermolysis, electrochemical oxidation, refluxing, ultrasonication and microwave [10]. On the other hand, a large number of quantum dots-based fluorescent probes have been recently applied to the detection of various metal ions [11–14]. For example, graphene quantum dots (GQD) was utilized to detect Cr6+ in water by quenching the fluorescence of GQDs [15]. Green fluorescent nitrogen-rich quantum dots (N-dots) served as a turn-off chemosensor for rapid and selective detection of Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solutions [16]. Otherwise, the fluorescent effects were found to be enhanced by doping CQDs with a variety of dopants including B or S [17–20].

With the rapid industrial development, heavy metal ion pollution has posed severe risks to organisms and environment [10] and become a worldwide problem. It is well known that silver ion (Ag+) plays an important role in electrical, photographic, and pharmaceutical industry. With the increase in the consumption of Ag+, about 2500t of silver is being released into environment every year [21], and its potential toxicity is drawing escalated concerns. It was reported that Ag+ in water could be accumulated by aquatic organisms, therefore poisoning aquatic animals, plants, and even human body [22]. And it was found that Ag+ could bind with various metabolites and enzymes to interfere with their normal functions [16]. Thus, highly selective detection of silver ion in aqueous media is extremely important in the context of environmental protection. On the other hand, cysteine (Cys), an essential amino acid, is widely involved with protein synthesis, detoxification, and metabolism and increased levels of Cys were associated with neurotoxicity [23]. Notably, Cys is a strong Ag+ binder and can remove Ag+ from C-Ag+-C (C = cytosine) base pairs, which could effectively turn off the fluorescence of sensor and exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for Cys.[24]

Herein, the objective of this research work is to develop an inexpensive method to synthesize multifunctional S,N-CQDs by using citric acid (CA) and thiamine hydrochloride (VB1). Remarkably, the prepared S,N-CQDs with a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) up to 63.82% served as a fluorescent signal-off and signal-on sensor and was successfully applied in the selective detection of Ag+ and Cys in aqueous medium respectively.

Thiamine hydrochloride, quinine sulfate and cysteine were bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, NaOH, H2SO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, AgNO3, Cu(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3·H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Zn(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, NiSO4, CoSO4, MgSO4, Al2(SO4)3, CaCl2, SrCl2, HgCl2, and NaNO3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical reagent grade. Amino acids (Hcy, Cys, Ala, Arg, His, Phe, Met, Pro, Tyr, Trp) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All Amino acids were biochemical reagent grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25MΩcm−1 by a Molecular water purification system.

Morphological evaluation and sizes of S,N-CQDs were analyzed using a HT7700 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi) under an accelerating voltage of 300kV. Elements of S,N-CQDs were detected under energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, Oxford). Surface functional properties of S,N-CQDs were investigated by PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific 250Xi). Infrared spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet by using IR Prestige-21 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence and absorption spectra were measured with a LS 55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and a UV-3600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively.

S,N-CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal protocol using CA and VB1 in Teflon-lined autoclave. Initially, 636.7mg of CA and 1.0066g of VB1 were dissolved in 25mL ultrapure water. And the mixture was then sonicated for 15min to give a transparent solution. The reaction mixture was then adjusted to neutral or alkaline with NaOH aqueous solution (2molL−1). Then the solution was transferred into a 50mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, heated at 160°C for 5h and then cooled down to room temperature. A brownish suspension was collected by removing large dots through centrifugation at 9000rpm for 15min. Finally, the suspension was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 100Da) for 24h while ultrapure water was changed in an interval of 8h. At last, the resulting solution was stored at 4°C.

Quantum yield (QY) of S,N-CQDs was measured according to the reported method [25]. Briefly, quinine sulfate (0.1M H2SO4 as solvent) was chosen as a reference standard (QY 54% at 365nm) and S,N-CQDs were dissolved in ultrapure water. Then the absorbance of sample was measured at the excitation wavelength of 370nm; the PL spectra of the solutions were then measured. Absolute values were calculated using the standard samples with a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value, according to the following Eq. (1) [26–28]:
(1)
Q
X
=
Q
S
(
Grad
X
/
Grad
S
) (
η
X
2
/
η
S
2
)
where subscripts S and X denote standard (quinine sulfate) and test samples (S,N-CQDs) respectively, Q is fluorescence quantum yield, Grad represents gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance, and η is refractive index of solvent.

The concentration of S,N-CQDs was adjusted to 10μgmL−1. Aqueous solutions containing fourteen different metal ions (1mM, Ag+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Hg2+) were prepared respectively, which were then subjected to fluorescence measurements after 5min. To study the detection range of Ag+, 2mL of S,N-CQDs solution (10μgmL−1 in 0.01M PBS, pH 7.0) was added to 3mL quartz cell, and then 4μL Ag+ solutions with varied concentrations were added to the system. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 424nm (excited at 370nm) after 3min. As shown in Fig. S1, Ag+ demonstrated same fluorescence quenching effect in either water or PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the concentration of Cys
ce:section.ce:para: 2mL of S,N-CQDs/Ag+ solution (10μgmL−1 in 0.01M PBS, pH 7.0) was added to a 3mL quartz cell. As for the detection of Cys, 4μL Cys solutions (0.01M PBS, pH 7.0) with varied concentrations were added to the system. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 424nm (excited at 370nm) after 3min.

HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 5 × 104 cellsmL−1 and cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 units per mL), and streptomycin (100 units per mL) at 37°C under a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 overnight. The cells were treated with S,N-CQDs (10–200μgmL−1) in 96-wells and then incubated for 24h. The cells attached to each hole were treated with 100μL of fresh DMEM containing 10μL methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5mgmL−1) after incubation, which were incubated again for 4h to form violet formazan. After removing extracellular free probes by washing for three times, 100μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve MTT. The resulting mixture was vibrated for 15min at room temperature in dark. Optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 490nm and the OD values were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Multiskan spectrum microplate spectrophotometer. The cell viability was calculated according to Eq.(2):[29]
(2)
Cell
viability
(
%
)
=
OD
treated
/
OD
control
× 100 %
where ODcontrol was acquired in the absence of S,N-CQDs and ODtreated was acquired in the presence of S,N-CQDs.

The potential of S,N-CQDs in bio-labeling was preliminarily evaluated by their cellular imaging in Hela cells. In brief, 100μL of Hela cells in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 units per mL), and streptomycin (100 units per mL) at an initial density of 5 × 104 cellsmL−1 were seeded in each hole and incubated at 37°C for 24h under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The dispersion of S,N-CQDs was prepared in DMEM medium with a concentration of 200μgmL−1. Cells were cultured with the dispersion of S,N-CQDs for 12h and washed with buffer for three times to remove free S,N-CQDs. Finally, the samples were observed with a laser scanning confocal microscope.

Analysis of practical water sample was carried out to evaluate the viability and practicality of the developed S,N-CQDs sensor. River water samples were collected from Xiangjiang River of Changsha (China). Tap water sample was collected from our laboratory. In order to eliminate the matrix effect, the obtained real water samples were filtered through 0.22μM filter membrane, and then diluted 5-fold with ultrapure water. To the samples of river water and tap water were added standard Ag+ solutions with different concentrations (0, 1, 2, and 4μM) and (0, 50, 100, and 150μM) respectively, which were analyzed by the developed S,N-CQDs sensor. 5mL of S,N-CQDs (20μgmL−1 in 0.01M PBS, pH 7.0) was added to 10mL volumetric flask, and 1mL river water and tap water containing Ag+ solutions with different concentrations (0, 1, 2, and 4μM) and (0, 50, 100, and 150μM) were then added to it respectively. The resulting mixture was diluted with PBS (0.01M, pH 7.0) to the designated volume and mixed, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 424nm (excited at 370nm) after 1min.

The human serums were obtained from XiangYa Hospital of Central South University. These samples were diluted 10-fold with PBS (0.01M, pH 7.0). Diluted serums samples (1mL) were added into S,N-CQDs and Ag+ solutions (10mgmL−1 and 10μM, respectively) with Cys with standard concentrations (the final concentration of Cys was 0, 0.5, 1, and 2μM). The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 424nm (excited at 370nm) after 1min.

The synthetic procedure of S,N-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1, in which CA was chosen as carbon source and VB1 acted as the sources of sulfur and nitrogen. Initially, direct coupling of citric acid and VB1 gave polymer-like S,N-CQDs, which were then carbonized to form the corresponding S,N-CQDs. It was found that there were some bulks insoluble in water after the reaction, which were unsuitable for fluorescence detection analysis. Therefore, the suspension was then centrifuged to remove insoluble bulks. The fluorescence efficiency of S,N-CQDs was affected by several factors including pH of reaction solution, molar ratio between sources of sulfur and nitrogen and carbon source, temperature of hydrothermal process, and reaction time. Consequently, these parameters were studied as shown in Fig. 1(b)-(e). As can be seen from Fig. 1(b), QYs of S,N-CQDs were greatly affected by pH value of the reaction, and the highest QY was acquired at pH 7. Presumably, the synthesis of S,N-CQDs performed in acidic or alkaline solutions stimulated the radiation and influenced the transition efficiency. According to Fig. 1(c), QY was very low in the absence of the source of sulfur and nitrogen while addition of VB1 significantly enhanced the corresponding QY. It was found that the optimum ratio between the source of sulfur and nitrogen and the carbon source was 1. Excess sulfur and nitrogen source would block the passivated surface defects, causing the decrease in QY. As shown in Fig. 1(d), carbonization degree was elevated at high temperature and the highest QY reached 63.82% at 160°C. As shown in Fig. 1(e), the highest QY can be easily found at 5h. Consequently, the optimum conditions were finalized as follows: pH 7, 0.01M CA, 0.01M VB1, 160°C and 5h.

In order to study the structure and morphology of the prepared S,N-CQDs, a series of characterizations including TEM, EDS, FT-IR, UV–Vis and XPS were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2(a), uniform particle size distribution of S,N-CQDs was observed, meanwhile the average particle size of S,N-CQDs was about 3nm (Fig. 2(b)). As shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d), EDS spectra confirmed the presence of sulfur and nitrogen in S,N-CQDs, verifying the success of the quantum-dots doping.

As shown in Fig. 2(e), FT-IR spectrum of S,N-CQDs revealed a band around 3343cm−1 and 3205cm−1 correlated with –OH and –NH stretching vibrations, respectively. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 2947cm−1 and 1410cm−1 represented –CH3 stretching vibration and C–H bending vibration [26], respectively. The peak at 1659cm−1 was attributed to C˭C, C˭O and C˭N stretching in the conjugated structure [30]. The peak at 1539cm−1 was attributed to C–N stretching vibration. The stretching vibration peak of S–H at 2360cm−1 and the characteristic absorption bands of C–S–C stretching vibration at 1211cm−1 demonstrated the presence of S and N elements on the surface of S,N-CQDs. The peak at 1049cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O–C bond. Presumably, all these functional groups and structural units mainly contributed to the excellent solubility of S,N-CQDs.

In UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2(f)), one absorption peak at 202nm could be attributed to n-σ* transition, indicating the presence of –NH2 and –SH. And two absorption peaks at 244 and 312nm could be assigned to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains including C˭C and C˭N bond and n-π* transition of C˭O bond [31,32], respectively.

The results of XPS analysis are shown in Fig. 3. Four distinct peaks around 164.0eV, 286.0eV, 399.0eV, 532.0eV in Fig. 3(a) could be ascribed to sulfur, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively [7]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), C1s spectrum could be divided into five main peaks corresponding to C˭C, C-C (284.6eV), C-S (285.2eV), C-N (286.2eV), C-O (286.7eV), and C˭N, C˭O (288.0eV) functional groups [26,33] respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(c), two peaks at 531.3 and 532.3eV in O1s spectrum can be attributed to C˭O and C-O-C/C-OH functional groups, respectively.[20,34] Deconvolution of N1s spectra in Fig. 3(d) revealed three relative nitrogen species of C˭N (398.2eV), -NH (399.1eV) and C-N-C (400.0eV).[34,35] The detailed information of sulfur could be characterized by the deconvoluted high resolution S2p spectrum in Fig. S2. The broad peak at lower binding energy consisted of three peaks, in which peaks at 163.8eV and 165.7eV were assigned to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2.[36]

The optical properties, ionic strength and pH of S,N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4. Fig. 4(a) shows the excitation spectrum (green line, λex = 370nm) and several emission spectra excited by the light with different wavelengths. It was manifested that S,N-CQDs exhibited strong emission with λem at 424nm, which was different from λex in a range from 310nm to 390nm. The pH-dependent behavior of S,N-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 4(b). High fluorescence intensity was observed with pH value ranging from 6 to 8 and the highest one was obtained at pH 7, suggesting that S,N-CQDs would function normally at physiologic pH. The fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs remained constant as the concentration of NaNO3 was increased even up to 2.0M (Fig. 4(c)), suggesting that S,N-CQDs had superior capability in anti-interference of ions. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the fluorescence intensity was still above 80% after 30 days under naturally lighting conditions. All these studies indicate that the prepared S,N-CQDs possess remarkable optical stability and are amenable to bio-sensing applications.[37]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of S,N-CQDs by Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence responses of S,N-CQDs upon addition of different metal ions were studied. As shown in Fig. 5(a), Ag+ showed the highest selectivity towards the fluorescence quenching of S,N-CQDs, implying that S,N-CQDs could be used to selectively detect Ag+ in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs at around 424nm decreased gradually with an increase in Ag+ concentration, indicating that addition of Ag+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs. Fig. S3 manifests the fluorescence intensities at different concentrations of Ag+. The fluorescence intensities of S,N-CQDs were weakened gradually as concentrations of Ag+ were varied from 0 to 250μM. As shown in Fig. 5(c), a good linear correlation (y = 1.2474 x – 0.0377) over the range from 10 to 250μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.990 was achieved. As shown in Fig. 5(d), a good linear correlation (y = 3.8393 x − 0.0676) over the range from 0 to 10μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.996 was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.4μM in a similar manner. (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is represents standard deviation of the blank S,N-CQDs sample recorded (N = 10) and S is the slope of the calibration curve [10,37]). The sensing performances of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection are summarized in Table 1. At this stage, it can be concluded that the prepared S,N-CQDs would provide a sensitive and convenient platform for the detection of Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of S,N-CQDs by Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent responses of S,N-CQDs/Ag+ solution (10μgmL−1 S,N-CQDs and 50μM Ag+) with the addition of various amino acids (Cys, Ala, Arg, His, Phe, Met, Pro, Tyr and Trp) were studied. As shown in Fig. 6(a), Cys demonstrates the highest fluorescence recovery of the mixture solution, implying the potential for S,N-CQDs as selective probe in the detection of Cys. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs/Ag+ at around 424nm increased gradually with Cys concentration increasing, indicating that addition of Cys could effectively recover the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs/Ag+. Fig. S4 manifests the fluorescence intensity of Cys with different concentrations, in which the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs/Ag+ increases gradually as the concentration of Cys ranges from 0 to 120μM. As shown in Fig. 6(c), a good linear correlation (y = 1.0503 x + 39.2193) over the range from 10 to 120μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.994 was achieved. As shown in Fig. 6(d), a good linear correlation (y = 4.2931 x − 0.9016) over the range from 0 to 10μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.991 was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.35μM in a similar manner. (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is represents standard deviation of the blank S,N-CQDs/Ag+ sample recorded (N = 10) and S is the slope of the calibration curve). Table 2 summarizes the sensing performances of different fluorescent probes for Cys detection.

The quenching-recovery mechanism is proposed as follows. Initially, Ag+ would facilely complex with sulfhydryl/amino groups on S,N-CQDs surfaces in the mixture solution of S,N-CQDs and Ag+. As a result, the structurally rigid planes of S,N-CQDs were distorted or destroyed [38], which subsequently suppressed the fluorescence by forming S,N-CQDs/Ag+ complexes. On the other hand, Cys exhibited stronger complexation capability towards Ag+ than S,N-CQDs. Consequently, Ag+ could be liberated from S,N-CQDs surfaces, leading to the recovery of fluorescence for S,N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity studies of S,N-CQDs and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of the prepared S,N-CQDs was evaluated via MTT assay, where HeLa cells were incubated with different amounts of S,N-CQDs for 24h. As shown in Fig. 7, over 90% cell viability was consistently observed for the tested S,N-CQDs solutions, showing a low cytotoxicity of S,N-CQDs.

The living cells were imaged with a confocal microscope under bright-field illumination ( Fig. 8(a)). The confocal image shows blue emission in HeLa cells under 370nm laser excitation (Fig. 8(b)). These results suggest that S,N-CQDs are able to pass through cell membranes to enter into cells, confirming the cell permeability of S,N-CQDs.

In order to exploit practical application of S,N-CQDs as fluorescence probes, standard addition experiments were performed with water and human serums samples to validate this method respectively. As shown in Table 3, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 0.87 and the recoveries were found to be varied between 97.0% and 107.0% for the samples from Xiangjiang River in Changsha. Besides, as for tap water, RSD (lower than 1.3) and recoveries (between 98.3% and 102.1%) were obtained. These results exemplifies the potential application of the S,N-CQDs-based fluorescent probe in the detection of Ag+ in aqueous samples. Moreover, as shown in Table 4, RSD was found to be lower than 1.1 with the recoveries between 94.0% and 112.0% for the detection of human serums. Moreover, the S,N-CQDs/Ag+-based sensing platform for Cys demonstrates high sensitivity in complicated biological matrix.

In conclusion, S,N co-doped CQDs with high chemical and optical stability were first synthesized in high quantum yield via a one-step hydrothermal protocol. The prepared S,N-CQDs were thoroughly characterized by chemical composition and structural analysis, confirming the presence of various sulfur and nitrogen relevant functional groups. The fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs was effectively quenched by Ag+ and then facilely recovered with the aid of Cystine. On the basis of the fluorescence quenching-recovery mechanism, S,N-CQDs served as an “off-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of silver ion and Cys in real samples with low detection limit and wide detection range. We believe that the development of S,N-CQDs might provide an effective solution for exploring inexpensive and highly sensitive and selective sensors in environmental detection and biological sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475152 & 21576296) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (No. 2017zzts339).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.12.040.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Ultrasmall organic nanoparticles with aggregation-induced emission and enhanced quantum yield for fluorescence cell imaging
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticle modulated turn-on fluorescent probes for histidine detection and its imaging in living cells
Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
High fluorescence S, N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid
Mesoporous TiO2 modified with carbon quantum dots as a high-performance visible light photocatalyst
